Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Alfred North Whitehead

1861 – 1947

Process, feeling, and a panexperiential metaphysics of mind.

Executive finding

Whitehead’s philosophy of mind is best reconstructed as a process-relational panexperientialism: actuality is not made of inert substances to which mind is later added, but of events or “actual occasions” whose basic operation is experiential prehension. Human mind is a high-grade, socially ordered, bodily supported stream of such occasions. Consciousness is not the essence of mind; it is a late, intermittent, contrastive, propositional achievement within experience. “Soul,” where Whitehead uses the term, is not a separable Cartesian substance but the coordinated personal route of experience sustained through bodily, social, and cosmic inheritance. God is not an optional theological appendix to this system: God functions as the ordering of relevant possibilities, the source of initial aim, and the consequent preservation of finite value.

The decisive reversal is this: Whitehead does not ask how consciousness emerges from wholly non-experiential matter. He thinks that question is malformed because “matter” conceived as purely vacuous, simply located stuff is already an abstraction. The concrete is event, relation, feeling, valuation, and inheritance. The task is then to explain how clear, self-conscious, reflective mentality emerges from more primitive feeling, not how feeling emerges from absolute non-feeling.

1. The problem Whitehead is trying to cure

Whitehead’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his critique of modern metaphysics. In Science and the Modern World, he argues that seventeenth-century scientific philosophy divided reality into two artificial halves: a mathematically described “real” nature of matter in motion and a merely apparent nature of color, sound, scent, warmth, value, and felt quality. Nature became “soundless, scentless, colourless,” while mind became the repository for whatever science’s abstraction had excluded.

This is the root of the modern mind-body problem. Once nature is defined as vacuous material extension and mind as private subjective awareness, philosophy is forced into unstable alternatives: dualism, materialist reduction, or idealism. Whitehead’s diagnosis is that all three usually mistake abstractions for concrete realities. He calls this the fallacy of misplaced concreteness: treating an abstract explanatory scheme as though it were the full concrete fact.

So Whitehead’s philosophy of mind begins by rejecting the inherited question, “How does mind get into matter?” His replacement question is: “What must concrete actuality be like, such that physical causation, bodily life, perception, value, mentality, consciousness, and God can all be treated within one categorial scheme?” The answer is his philosophy of organism.

2. Actual occasions: the units of reality are experiential events

Whitehead’s basic metaphysical unit is the actual entity or actual occasion. Actual entities are the final real things of which the world is composed; they are not substances bearing qualities but momentary processes of becoming. Whitehead famously describes them as “drops of experience,” though their degrees of experience differ vastly, from the most trivial event to God.

An actual occasion does not endure as an unchanged subject. It becomes, achieves its satisfaction, perishes, and then functions as datum for later occasions. Whitehead’s subject is therefore a subject-superject: subject during its own concrescence, superject when it has become an objective fact for successors. He explicitly rejects the model of an unchanged subject undergoing change; in his system, no thinker thinks twice, because each act of thinking is a new occasion inheriting from prior occasions.

This is crucial for philosophy of mind. The “self” is not a persisting mental substance behind its experiences. It is a serially ordered society of experiential occasions, each inheriting from the previous personal occasion and from the wider bodily world. Personal identity is continuity of inheritance, pattern, memory, aim, and bodily-social organization, not numerical identity of a substance that remains unchanged through time.

3. Prehension: the basic operation of mind and world

The central concept for extracting Whitehead’s philosophy of mind is prehension. A prehension is an act of taking account of, feeling, or incorporating a datum. It is not necessarily conscious, cognitive, or voluntary. It is the concrete relational operation by which one actual occasion receives, transforms, includes, excludes, or values another actuality or possibility. Whitehead gives prehension a vector character: it carries the force of the past into the becoming present and includes emotion, purpose, valuation, and causation.

A prehension has three factors: a subject that prehends, a datum that is prehended, and a subjective form, meaning the way the datum is felt. This matters because the same world is not simply copied into experience; it is felt under a mode, tone, relevance, valuation, or exclusion. Whitehead thereby gives a metaphysical analysis of what later philosophy might call affectivity, intentionality, salience, and perspective.

Prehension is also Whitehead’s answer to the problem of causal connection. Causation is not an external push between self-contained substances. It is the inheritance of one occasion by another. The past is literally felt by the present. This is why Whitehead can say that experience is not locked inside a private mental chamber: experience is constituted by its relation to what it receives.

4. Physical and mental poles: not dualism, but dipolarity

Every actual occasion has a physical pole and a mental pole. The physical pole prehends actual entities: the settled facts of the past. The mental pole prehends eternal objects: pure possibilities, forms, patterns, alternatives, or potential determinations. This is not mind-body dualism because the poles are not two substances. They are two abstractable aspects of one concrescent event.

The physical pole gives inheritance, causal continuity, emotional tone, and the weight of the past. The mental pole introduces valuation of possibilities, contrast, novelty, and aim. Mentality, in this technical sense, is not the same as consciousness. Whitehead says the mental pole has no necessary connection with consciousness; consciousness is only one possible subjective form of some high-grade feelings.

The philosophy of mind that follows is subtle. Whitehead is not saying electrons are conscious, nor that stones have minds in the human sense. He is saying that actuality is never merely vacuous. Even the lowest occasions have some primitive mode of feeling or valuation, while consciousness appears only in complex, high-grade organizations. This is why many interpreters prefer panexperientialism over panpsychism: experience is basic, but consciousness is not universal.

5. Consciousness is derivative, not fundamental

Whitehead’s most important thesis for philosophy of consciousness is explicit: consciousness presupposes experience; experience does not presuppose consciousness. Consciousness is a special element in the subjective form of some feelings, not the essence of feeling as such.

This reverses much of modern philosophy. Descartes begins from clear conscious awareness. Whitehead thinks that clarity is late and derivative. The primitive basis of experience is bodily, causal, emotional, vague, efficacious, and only partly illuminated by consciousness. The Spanish-language study by Alfonso Gómez captures this point well: in Whitehead, consciousness and thought are derivative; consciousness arises through contrast and only vaguely illuminates primitive causal prehensions, whereas it more clearly illuminates later presentational modes.

Whitehead’s technical account is that consciousness arises when physical feelings and conceptual feelings are integrated in a way that contrasts fact with possibility. It is not mere sensation, nor mere thought, but a subjective form of contrast: this actual fact as felt against what might be, what might not be, or what is conceptually entertained.

This gives Whitehead a powerful account of why consciousness is selective. Conscious awareness does not reveal the full depth of experience. It spotlights contrasts, alternatives, negations, propositions, and possibilities. The deeper causal-emotional inheritance of the body and world mostly remains below clear consciousness, though it continuously shapes what consciousness can notice.

6. Propositions, contrast, and the structure of conscious awareness

For Whitehead, consciousness is bound to propositional feeling. A proposition is not first of all a sentence. It is a lure for feeling: a pattern of possibility entertained in relation to actual facts. Consciousness requires something like affirmation, negation, or contrast; it involves a felt comparison between what is given and what could be.

This means that consciousness is intrinsically modal. It is awareness not only of actuality but of actuality under the pressure of possibility. A conscious organism does not merely register “red,” “danger,” or “pain.” It feels contrasts: this red as object, this danger as avoidable, this pain as to be endured or relieved, this action as possible, this future as open.

Whitehead’s philosophy of consciousness is therefore neither a raw-feel theory nor a purely representational theory. Consciousness has qualitative, affective, causal, bodily, and propositional dimensions. It is not exhausted by representation, but it also is not a mute quale. It is a structured feeling of contrast.

7. Perception: causal efficacy, presentational immediacy, and symbolic reference

Whitehead’s theory of perception is one of the most important parts of his philosophy of mind. He distinguishes two direct modes of perception: causal efficacy and presentational immediacy. Presentational immediacy is the clear sensory display of a contemporary world: colors, shapes, spatial surfaces, visual patterns. Causal efficacy is the more primitive bodily feeling of being affected by the past: the pressure of the body, the weight of causal inheritance, the vague but forceful sense that the world is acting upon us.

Modern empiricism usually privileges presentational immediacy because it is clear. Whitehead thinks this is backwards. The clear is often derivative. Causal efficacy is more primitive and metaphysically deeper. Primitive organisms, bodily moods, fear in darkness, and visceral awareness reveal that we are not first spectators of visual surfaces; we are inheritors of causal forces.

Symbolic reference fuses the two modes. In ordinary perception, we do not merely see patches of color, nor merely feel causal pressure. We see the car as dangerous, the face as angry, the chair as sit-able, the road as crossable or not. Whitehead’s example of crossing a road shows perception as a practical synthesis of sensory presentation and causal meaning. Experience arises from the past, enriches itself with emotion and purpose, and then passes its character to the future.

The result is a realist philosophy of perception without naïve simplification. The world is not inferred from private sense-data; it is already efficaciously present in experience. But what is consciously perceived is also shaped by symbolic integration, bodily scale, selective emphasis, and conceptual interpretation.

8. Body, brain, and the living person

Whitehead’s philosophy of mind is radically embodied. He rejects the “detached mind” as an abstraction. Mental activity is a mode of feeling belonging to actual entities in some degree, but conscious intellectuality appears only in some high-grade occasions.

In Modes of Thought, Whitehead says the proper procedure is not to begin with “body” and “mind” as two independent facts, but with the human individual as one fact. Human experience has two sources: the body and antecedent experiential functionings. While we exist, body and soul are inescapable elements in our being.

The body is not a machine externally connected to a mind. It is a vast society of occasions whose activities are inherited by the personal stream. The soul is not an immaterial substance added to the body; Whitehead describes it as a coordinated stream of personal experiences. Its continuity is real but not substance-like. It has gaps, vagueness, sleep, unconsciousness, and dependence upon the body’s organized processes.

In Process and Reality, Whitehead analyzes the living person as an enduring entity rather than as the essence of life itself. Personal mentality evolves with the material organism; higher animals have central direction; human self-consciousness is direct awareness of ourselves as such persons. He also notes pathological cases, dissociation, multiple personalities, and forms of religious pathology, which shows that personal unity is an achievement, not a guaranteed metaphysical simple.

Whitehead’s most striking neurophilosophical claim is his theory of a presiding occasion or central personal dominance. In higher animals, the mind is not identical with the whole brain, nor detached from it. It is a serially ordered route of high-grade occasions inheriting from the bodily-brain nexus. Whitehead even says the route of the presiding occasion may “wander” from part to part of the brain. This is speculative, but it is meant to avoid both reductionism and dualism: the mind is an emergent dominant route within the body’s living society, not a ghost outside it.

9. Soul: what Whitehead can and cannot mean

Whitehead’s “soul” should be read as personal order, not as a simple immortal substance. The soul is the historical route of personal occasions, each receiving the past and transmitting itself to the future. It is a society with personal order, a stream of experience coordinated through bodily life.

This gives a non-Cartesian account of selfhood. The self is neither an illusion nor a substance. It is a real pattern of inheritance and self-identification. Its unity is temporal, social, bodily, and purposive. It exists by repetition with difference: each new personal occasion inherits from its predecessor but also makes a fresh decision.

This also means that Whitehead’s account of the soul is vulnerable to bodily disruption. Sleep, unconsciousness, brain injury, dissociation, and psychopathology are not superficial disturbances of an untouched inner substance. They can alter the personal route itself. Whitehead’s own discussion of multiple personalities and the possible weakening or vanishing of central personal dominance shows that he takes such cases metaphysically seriously.

The theological question of immortality is more delicate. Whitehead clearly gives every actual occasion objective immortality: once it has become, it is a fact for the future and is also preserved in God’s consequent nature. But that is not automatically the same as the continuing subjective survival of an ego after death. A careful Whiteheadian view should therefore distinguish preservation of value in God from personal afterlife doctrines developed by later process theologians.

10. Life, mentality, and the unconscious

Whitehead distinguishes life from mentality. In Modes of Thought, life involves self-enjoyment, creative activity, and aim. Mentality, more narrowly, involves conceptual experience: the entertainment of possibilities. Mentality may be latent in inorganic occasions and becomes increasingly effective in higher forms of life, especially higher mammals and human beings.

This gives Whitehead a metaphysics of the unconscious. Most experience is not conscious. Bodily feelings, causal inheritances, emotional valuations, appetitions, and primitive conceptual contrasts can operate without reflective awareness. Consciousness is a high-grade illumination within a much broader field of feeling.

This is not Freud’s unconscious, though it can converse with depth psychology. Whitehead’s unconscious is metaphysical before it is clinical. It is the nonconscious functioning of prehensions, aims, valuations, bodily inheritances, and symbolic integrations that make conscious experience possible.

11. Emotion and value are not secondary ornaments

Whitehead’s philosophy of mind is affective at its root. Prehensions involve subjective forms; subjective forms include emotional tone, valuation, aversion, attraction, intensity, and purpose. In Modes of Thought, Whitehead explicitly treats purposive experience as part of nature, not as an epiphenomenal illusion. We are directly conscious of purposes as directive of actions, and mental operations are not “idle wheels.”

This is why Whitehead is not a neutral information-processing theorist. Feeling is not data plus a later affective coloring. Feeling is the primitive way in which data become relevant for a subject. Value is built into the becoming of actual occasions because each occasion aims at a satisfaction, a determinate unity of its inherited world.

This also explains Whitehead’s importance for philosophy of action. Agency is not an uncaused act by a self-subsistent ego. It is a process of self-determination within inherited conditions. The past is efficient cause; the subject’s aim and decision are final-causal. An occasion is partly caused by its world and partly self-creative in how it integrates that world.

12. Freedom, decision, and subjective aim

Whitehead’s account of freedom is neither strict determinism nor arbitrary indeterminism. Each occasion receives a determinate past, but the past underdetermines the final satisfaction. The occasion must decide how to integrate, emphasize, exclude, value, and complete its data. This self-completion is its concrescence.

The subjective aim is the directedness of this process toward a determinate satisfaction. In human experience, subjective aim appears as desire, purpose, attention, project, valuation, and practical orientation. In lower occasions, it is vastly more primitive, but the categorial structure is continuous.

Whitehead’s freedom is therefore graded. Human freedom is not metaphysically unique, but it is enormously more complex because human occasions entertain richer contrasts, wider possibilities, symbolic structures, memories, language, and social inheritances. Freedom increases with intensity and complexity of mentality.

13. God and mind: theology as part of the philosophy of consciousness

Whitehead’s theological claims are not detachable embellishments. God is an actual entity in the same ultimate categorial scheme, though not a temporal actual occasion like finite occasions. God’s role is central to possibility, relevance, value, aim, and preservation.

God has a primordial nature and a consequent nature. The primordial nature is conceptual: God orders eternal objects, the realm of possibilities, in terms of relevance. This is not yet divine consciousness in the ordinary sense; Whitehead describes the primordial nature as conceptual and unconscious. The consequent nature is God’s physical prehension of the actual world; it is conscious, responsive, and enriched by finite becoming.

This matters for philosophy of mind because it makes mentality cosmic without making finite minds omniscient fragments of a single mind. God is not the universal substance of which minds are modes. Rather, God is the primordial ordering of possibility and the consequent reception of actuality. Divine mind is relational and affected by the world.

God also supplies the initial aim for each finite occasion: the lure toward a possible form of satisfaction. This is not coercive efficient causation. It is persuasive relevance, the presentation of a best possibility relative to the occasion’s actual situation.

The consequent nature of God is equally important. Whitehead’s God does not create the world in the classical sense; God saves it, preserves it, and works as the poet of the world by leading through truth, beauty, and goodness. The world’s perished occasions are not lost; they are taken into divine experience and become part of an everlasting order of value.

For philosophy of mind, this yields a theologically inflected account of memory, value, and immortality. Human subjective continuity may perish, but finite experience is not metaphysically annihilated. It has objective immortality in future occasions and divine preservation in God’s consequent nature.

14. Whitehead’s answer to the hard problem

Whitehead’s answer to the hard problem of consciousness is not a solution within the assumptions that generate the hard problem. He denies the premise that concrete reality is fundamentally non-experiential. The “hard problem” becomes hard because one starts with vacuous matter and then tries to derive feeling. Whitehead starts with feeling-like actuality at the base, then explains consciousness as a special intensification of feeling through contrast, conceptuality, and bodily organization.

This does not eliminate every difficulty. It shifts the burden. A Whiteheadian must explain grades of experience, the emergence of unified high-grade subjects, the relation between neural societies and presiding occasions, and the nature of consciousness as contrast. But the explanatory gap is not between absolute non-experience and experience. It is between primitive experience and reflective consciousness.

15. The combination problem and Whitehead’s “dominant occasion” strategy

Panpsychist theories often face the combination problem: how do many micro-experiences combine into one macro-conscious subject? Whitehead’s answer is not simple summation. A living body is a society of occasions, but a high-grade personal occasion can prehend and integrate bodily data into a new unity. The conscious subject is not a heap of cellular or neuronal feelings; it is a dominant or presiding occasion arising within, and depending upon, the bodily-brain nexus.

This is one of the boldest and most controversial parts of the theory. Its advantage is that it avoids saying that many little subjects magically add up to one big subject. Its cost is that it posits high-grade occasions associated with living bodily organization, and Whitehead’s exact account of their relation to neurophysiology remains speculative.

Still, the view is philosophically precise: unity of consciousness is not aggregation; it is a new concrescent unity prehending many data. A person is not identical with the whole body but depends on a living bodily society whose activities supply the material inherited by the personal stream.

16. Intentionality, representation, and meaning

Whitehead’s account of intentionality is broader than modern representationalism. Every prehension is “of” something in the minimal sense that it has a datum. But the datum is not merely represented; it is felt, inherited, transformed, valued, and integrated. The basic intentional relation is therefore prehensive, not pictorial.

At higher levels, propositions become lures for feeling. A proposition presents a possible predication of actual entities. It can be entertained, valued, believed, disbelieved, acted upon, or rejected. Conscious intentionality emerges when such propositional contrasts become part of experience.

This gives Whitehead a layered theory of meaning. Meaning is not merely linguistic reference. It begins as relevance in feeling, becomes symbolic reference in perception, becomes propositional lure in thought, and becomes explicit judgment in consciousness.

17. Knowledge: not copying but prehending

Whitehead’s epistemology follows his metaphysics. Knowing is not the passive copying of an external object by an inner mind. It is a specialized form of prehension. The Spanish article on knowledge and process emphasizes that, for Whitehead, knowledge is not mimesis or copy but prehension: an actual entity takes account of others, objectifies them at its own scale, and integrates them into its own process.

This makes Whitehead a realist, but not a simple representational realist. The object is genuinely received; it is not generated by the subject. Yet reception is always selective, affective, scalar, and perspectival. Knowledge is a real operation upon the real, not a detached mirror.

French discussions help clarify this point. Xavier Verley’s comparison of Whitehead and Merleau-Ponty argues that Whitehead treats perception as prehension or appropriation, whereas Merleau-Ponty gives nature, life, and body reality mediated by consciousness. This brings out Whitehead’s distinctive realism: consciousness does not constitute nature; rather, consciousness is one derivative factor within nature’s experiential relations.

18. Multilingual reception: useful accents outside the standard Anglo frame

The French reception often stresses Whitehead’s anti-bifurcation project and his relevance to phenomenology, Bergson, Merleau-Ponty, and philosophies of life. Isabelle Stengers’ reception, as discussed in French, emphasizes that when Whitehead becomes a metaphysician, neither “nature” nor “mind” survives as an independent ultimate category; knowledge is no longer the final philosophical court of appeal.

Another French source, comparing Whitehead and Merleau-Ponty, says consciousness is only the last and greatest selective element by which the individual masks the external totality from which it arises and which it embodies. That is an especially useful gloss on Whiteheadian consciousness: consciousness clarifies by selecting, but it also obscures by narrowing.

French scholarship also connects Whitehead to Hans Jonas. A Persée article notes that Jonas’s references to Whitehead were not signs of ignorance or indifference; Jonas devoted a substantial New School course to Whitehead and especially Process and Reality in 1970. This matters because it places Whitehead’s philosophy of organism in conversation with twentieth-century philosophy of life, organism, and responsibility.

German reception often frames the issue as the Leib-Seele-Problem, the body-soul problem. A German bibliography describes David Ray Griffin’s Unsnarling the World-Knot as a major elaboration of Whitehead’s solution in terms of panpsychism or panexperientialism. This is a different emphasis from some Anglo analytic treatments: it keeps “soul” and “body” in view rather than translating everything immediately into “mind-body physicalism.”

Spanish-language scholarship is particularly helpful on prehension and consciousness. Gómez’s study explains prehensions as relational bonds linking actual entities with each other and with eternal objects, and it stresses that consciousness, knowledge, and thought are rooted in a prior process that constitutes them. This is a strong reconstruction of Whitehead as a philosopher of preconscious process rather than a philosopher of reflective consciousness.

Chinese-language discussions show an active contemporary reception around process philosophy, comparative philosophy, ecology, and even AI. Chinese Social Sciences Network reports recent conferences comparing Whitehead with the Yijing, discussing process cosmology, ecological civilization, and Whitehead’s influence on Chinese philosophers. A 2024 report also notes a presentation connecting Whitehead’s cognitive and prehensive framework to contemporary artificial intelligence.

The broader institutional point is also significant: the Stanford Encyclopedia notes substantial Chinese engagement with process thought, including many process centers founded in the People’s Republic of China between 2002 and 2018.

19. A reconstructed Whiteheadian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Whitehead can be stated in the following theses.

First, actuality is experiential event, not inert substance. The final units of reality are occasions of becoming. Human experience is not an anomaly in a dead universe; it is a high-grade form of a universal categorial pattern.

Second, experience is broader than consciousness. Feeling, prehension, valuation, and aim are metaphysically basic. Consciousness is a derivative mode of some high-grade feelings. Therefore Whitehead is a panexperientialist, not a panconsciousness theorist.

Third, mind is dipolar. Every occasion has physical inheritance from actualities and mental valuation of possibilities. Human mind is the intensified, organized, conscious form of this dipolar structure.

Fourth, the body is constitutive of mind. Human mentality is not inserted into a body from outside. It arises as a personal route of high-grade occasions within a living bodily-brain society. The body supplies the inherited material of personal experience.

Fifth, the soul is a personal stream, not a substance. The soul is the coordinated continuity of personal experiences, with gaps, vulnerabilities, dependence on bodily organization, and real historical identity through inheritance.

Sixth, perception is direct but layered. We directly perceive through causal efficacy and presentational immediacy, integrated by symbolic reference. The world is not hidden behind private sense-data; it is felt causally before it is clearly represented.

Seventh, consciousness is contrastive and propositional. Consciousness arises when fact and possibility are felt together in contrast. It is tied to affirmation, negation, alternatives, and lures for feeling.

Eighth, agency is self-determination under inheritance. Each occasion receives its past but completes itself through decision. Human freedom is the complex form of this general process, enriched by conceptuality, language, memory, and social life.

Ninth, value is ontological. Each occasion aims at satisfaction; experience is intrinsically evaluative. Beauty, importance, intensity, harmony, and contrast are not merely subjective projections but belong to the structure of becoming.

Tenth, God is the cosmic condition of relevance and preservation. God orders possibilities, provides initial aims, receives the world into divine experience, and preserves finite achievement. Theology is therefore part of Whitehead’s account of mentality, value, and immortality.

20. What Whitehead is not saying

Whitehead is not a Cartesian dualist. He does not posit two independent substances, mind and matter, which must somehow interact. His physical and mental poles are aspects of actual occasions, not separately existing worlds.

He is not a reductive materialist. Matter as vacuous actuality is an abstraction, not the concrete basis from which mind must be reduced. The concrete world includes feeling, value, relation, and aim.

He is not a subjective idealist. Actual occasions receive real antecedent entities; the object is not merely generated by the subject. Whitehead’s reformed subjectivism begins from experience but finds other actualities objectified within experience.

He is not saying everything is conscious. Consciousness is rare, derivative, and dependent on complex organization. Primitive experience is not reflective awareness.

He is not offering a conventional doctrine of the immortal soul. He offers objective immortality and divine preservation of value; personal subjective survival would require further argument beyond the minimal claims of the system.

21. Open difficulties

The first difficulty is empirical: Whitehead’s theory of presiding occasions is metaphysically suggestive but not directly integrated with contemporary neuroscience. A Whiteheadian philosophy of mind needs a careful bridge between neural dynamics and the serial route of dominant occasions.

The second difficulty is the unity problem. Whitehead avoids simple aggregation by positing high-grade concrescent unities, but critics may ask whether this explains or merely redescribes the unity of consciousness.

The third difficulty concerns God. Whitehead’s God elegantly supplies relevance, aim, and preservation, but some philosophers will ask whether the system can function without God, or whether eternal objects and initial aims require the divine primordial nature. A strictly secular Whiteheadianism is possible, but it is not obviously identical with Whitehead’s own mature system.

The fourth difficulty is terminology. Whitehead’s words—prehension, concrescence, subjective form, satisfaction, eternal object, nexus—are not decorative jargon. They mark technical distinctions. But they also make his philosophy difficult to translate into contemporary philosophy of mind without distortion.

22. Final synthesis

Whitehead’s philosophy of mind is a theory of embodied, affective, processual subjectivity. Mind is not a thing but a route of occasions. Consciousness is not the ground of experience but an achievement within experience. The soul is not an immaterial substance but the historical coordination of personal becoming. Perception is not an inner copy but a bodily-causal-symbolic participation in the world. Agency is not exemption from causality but self-determining integration of inherited fact and relevant possibility. God is not an external supernatural interruption but the cosmic ordering of possibility and preservation of achieved value.

The most compact formula is this: the mind is the world becoming personally relevant to itself through a living body, under the lure of possibility, with consciousness emerging where fact and possibility are felt in contrast.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
